title: Cat Pulling Fur Out of Belly: Causes and Veterinary Triage
meta_desc: Is your cat over-grooming their stomach? Learn about common causes, urgency levels, and why a photo helps your vet diagnose the issue.
content_html:
TL;DR: Belly-pulling in cats is usually caused by stress, allergies, or pain and is typically a chronic issue rather than an emergency, unless the skin is infected or the cat cannot urinate.

Why is my cat pulling fur from its belly?

When a cat pulls fur from its belly, the condition is medically referred to as psychogenic alopecia or traumatic hair loss. This occurs when a cat excessively grooms an area, often leading to thinning fur, bald patches, or even skin irritation. This behavior can be triggered by physiological issues like allergies or bladder pain, or psychological factors such as stress and anxiety.

Is belly-pulling in cats an emergency?

  • Urgency Level: Low. In most cases, belly-pulling is a chronic issue rather than an acute emergency.
  • Seek veterinary care sooner if the skin is bleeding or oozing.
  • Contact a vet immediately if your cat is also struggling to urinate.
  • A scheduled appointment within a few days is appropriate for simple hair loss without open wounds.

How does a photo help a veterinarian triage my cat's hair loss?

  • Allows a veterinary professional to see the exact pattern of the hair loss.
  • Provides a clear view of the skin underneath to check for inflammation or signs of allergy.
  • Helps confirm active over-grooming by showing if the hair shafts are broken.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In cats presenting with fur pulling from the belly, underlying medical conditions causing pruritus or pain should be investigated. Common causes include infectious skin diseases such as bacterial pyoderma and dermatophytosis, ectoparasites, and allergic skin diseases like atopic dermatitis, food allergy, contact allergy, and insect hypersensitivity. Less common causes include neoplastic skin diseases. Flea allergy dermatitis is a frequent cause of symmetric alopecia in cats, and a complete blood count with differential may reveal eosinophilia, even in the absence of obvious flea infestation. Compulsive disorders should be considered if medical causes are ruled out, especially if grooming behaviors occur with excessive frequency or duration.

Chapter: Behavioral Medicine, Dermatology, Neurology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1574)

faqs.question: Can stress cause my cat to pull out their belly fur?
faqs.answer: Yes, cats often use grooming as a displacement behavior to self-soothe during stressful events, such as moving house or the addition of a new pet.

faqs.question: Could my cat be in pain?
faqs.answer: Possibly. Cats sometimes over-groom the skin directly above a source of internal pain, such as the bladder if they are experiencing cystitis.

faqs.question: How do I stop my cat from over-grooming until the vet visit?
faqs.answer: Try to minimize environmental stressors and avoid scolding the cat, as this can increase anxiety. Using a recovery suit or an Elizabethan collar can provide a temporary physical barrier.
